content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 1 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 195–197 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_001_page_001.jpg, DEU_001_page_002.jpg, DEU_001_page_003.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 143–144

---

1 These [are] the words that Moshe spoke to all Yisra'el beyond the Yarden, in the wilderness, in the Arabah, opposite Suph, between Paran and Tophel and Lavan and Chatserot and Di-zahav —

2 eleven days from Horeb by the way of Mount Se'ir to Kadesh-barnea.

3 And it was in the fortieth year, in the eleventh month, on the first of the month, Moshe spoke to the sons of Yisra'el according to all that the LORD had commanded him to them —

4 after his striking Sichon king of the Emori who dwelt in Heshbon, and Og king of the Bashan who dwelt in Ashtarot, in Edre'i.

5 Beyond the Yarden, in the land of Mo'av, Moshe began to expound this Torah, saying:

6 The LORD our God spoke to us in Horeb, saying: "You have dwelt long enough at this mountain.

7 Turn and journey and go to the mountain of the Emori and to all its neighbors, in the Arabah, in the hill-country and in the lowland and in the Negev and on the coast of the sea — the land of the Kena'ani and the Levanon, as far as the great river, the river Perat.

8 See — I have placed before you the land. Go in and possess the land that the LORD swore to your fathers, to Avraham, to Yitschak, and to Ya'akov, to give to them and to their seed after them."

9 And I spoke to you at that time, saying: "I am not able to carry you alone.

10 The LORD your God has multiplied you, and behold, you [are] today as the stars of the heavens in multitude.

11 The LORD, the God of your fathers, may he add to you a thousand times more than you [are], and may he bless you as he has spoken to you.

12 How can I alone carry your burden and your load and your striving?

13 Provide for yourselves men, wise and discerning and knowing, for your tribes, and I will set them as your heads."

14 And you answered me and said: "The word that you have spoken [is] good to do."

15 And I took the heads of your tribes, men wise and known, and I set them as heads over you — commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds and commanders of fifties and commanders of tens and officers for your tribes.

16 And I commanded your judges at that time, saying: "Hear between your brothers and judge with righteousness between a man and his brother and his sojourner.

17 You shall not show partiality in judgment: the small as the great you shall hear; you shall not fear any man's face, for the judgment [belongs] to God. And the matter that is too hard for you, you shall bring near to me and I will hear it."

18 And I commanded you at that time all the things that you shall do.

19 And we journeyed from Horeb and went through all that great and fearful wilderness that you saw — the way to the mountain of the Emori — as the LORD our God commanded us, and we came to Kadesh-barnea.

20 And I said to you: "You have come to the mountain of the Emori that the LORD our God gives to us.

21 See — the LORD your God has placed before you the land. Go up, possess [it], as the LORD, the God of your fathers, has spoken to you. Do not fear and do not be dismayed."

22 And you drew near to me, all of you, and said: "Let us send men before us and let them search the land for us, and they will bring back to us word of the way by which we shall go up and the cities into which we shall come."

23 And the matter was good in my eyes, and I took from you twelve men, one man per tribe.

24 And they turned and went up to the hill-country and came to the Wadi Eshkol and spied it out.

25 And they took in their hands from the fruit of the land and brought [it] down to us, and they brought back to us word, and said: "Good [is] the land that the LORD our God gives to us."

26 But you were not willing to go up, and you rebelled against the command of the LORD your God.

27 And you murmured in your tents and said: "In the LORD's hatred of us he has brought us out of the land of Mitsrayim, to give us into the hand of the Emori to destroy us.

28 To where are we going up? Our brothers have made our hearts melt, saying: 'A people greater and taller than us, cities great and fortified to the heavens, and also sons of the Anakim — we saw there.'"

29 And I said to you: "Do not be terrified and do not fear them.

30 The LORD your God who goes before you — he himself will fight for you, according to all that he did with you in Mitsrayim before your eyes,

31 and in the wilderness, where you saw that the LORD your God carried you, as a man carries his son, on all the way that you went until your coming to this place."

32 But in this matter you do not trust in the LORD your God,

33 who goes before you on the way — to seek out for you a place to encamp in — in fire by night, to show you in which way to go, and in cloud by day.

34 And the LORD heard the voice of your words and was angry, and he swore, saying:

35 "If any man of these men of this evil generation see the good land that I swore to give to your fathers —

36 except Kalev son of Yephunneh — he shall see it, and to him I will give the land that he trod upon, and to his sons, because he wholly followed after the LORD."

37 Also against me the LORD showed anger on your account, saying: "Even you shall not enter there.

38 Yehoshua son of Nun who stands before you — he shall enter there. Strengthen him, for he shall cause Yisra'el to inherit it.

39 And your little ones who you said would be for plunder, and your children who this day have no knowledge of good or evil — they shall enter there, and to them I will give it and they shall possess it.

40 But as for you, turn and journey into the wilderness by the way of the Reed Sea."

41 And you answered and said to me: "We have sinned against the LORD. We ourselves will go up and fight, according to all that the LORD our God commanded us." And each man girded his weapons of war and treated [it as] easy to go up to the hill-country.

42 And the LORD said to me: "Say to them: Do not go up and do not fight, for I am not in your midst, lest you be struck before your enemies."

43 And I spoke to you, but you would not listen; and you rebelled against the command of the LORD, and you acted presumptuously and went up to the hill-country.

44 And the Emori who dwelt in that hill-country came out to meet you and chased you as the bees do, and they struck you in Se'ir as far as Hormah.

45 And you returned and wept before the LORD, but the LORD did not hear your voice and did not give ear to you.

46 And you dwelt in Kadesh many days, as the days that you dwelt [there].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.1 — elleh ha-devarim ("these [are] the words"):** The opening of Devarim (Deuteronomy) takes its Hebrew name from this phrase. The LXX reads *Οὗτοι οἱ λόγοι* — exact correspondence. The phrase functions as both the book's title and its genre marker: a covenant-renewal speech. The archaic geographical list that follows (Suph, Tophel, Lavan, Chatserot, Di-zahav) has no clear identification; I render them as proper names preserving the obscurity of the MT. The LXX renders Suph as *Σούφ*, Di-zahav as *Καταχρύσεα* ("golden region") — an interpretive gloss not in MT.

**v.1 — be-ever ha-yarden ("beyond the Yarden"):** This phrase is directionally ambiguous in the MT and has generated much scholarly discussion. From the standpoint of the narrator/compiler in Canaan, it means "east of the Jordan." EMET preserves "beyond" without clarifying the direction.

**v.2 — eleven days:** The LXX (p. 143) preserves this reading identically (*ἕνδεκα ἡμερῶν*). The note is poignant: a journey of eleven days took forty years due to disobedience.

**v.3 — eleventh month:** Vaticanus has *ἑνδεκάτῳ μηνί* — exact. The fortieth year is 1406 BCE in traditional chronology, 1 Shevat.

**v.5 — ho'il Moshe be'er ("Moshe began to expound"):** The verb *be'er* (Pi'el of *ba'ar*) means to make clear, to dig out, to expound. It is unique in the Torah and marks Deuteronomy's character as a didactic exposition rather than simple narrative. LXX: *διασαφῆσαι* (to make plain).

**v.7 — Perat:** The great Euphrates. I preserve the Hebrew form rather than the Greek "Euphrates."

**v.13 — wise and discerning and knowing:** Three Hebrew adjectives — *chakhamim* (wise), *nevonim* (discerning, understanding), *yedua'im* (known, recognized). The LXX renders the third as *γνωστούς* (known to you), suggesting the sense of "men well known in your communities." EMET follows MT order.

**v.17 — for the judgment belongs to God:** *ki ha-mishpat le-Elohim hu* — a remarkable theological statement embedded in juridical instruction. God is the ultimate judge; human judges are his delegates. LXX: *ὅτι ἡ κρίσις τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστιν* — exact correspondence.

**v.27 — in the LORD's hatred of us:** *be-sin'at YHWH otanu* — literally "in the hating of YHWH us." The people attribute their exodus to divine hatred, an ironic inversion of the love-motivated exodus theology. The LXX softens nothing: *διὰ τὸ μισεῖν Κύριον ἡμᾶς*.

**v.31 — as a man carries his son:** *ka'asher yissa' ish et-beno* — a paternal image for divine care rarely paralleled in the Pentateuch. The LXX (p. 144) renders faithfully: *ὡς εἴ τις τροφοφορήσει ἄνθρωπος τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ*. The verb *trophophoreo* means to nurse/carry as a nurse — an even more intimate image than the MT.

**v.36 — Kalev son of Yephunneh:** The exception clause for Kalev is precise: *ya'an asher mille' acharei YHWH* — "because he wholly followed after the LORD." The phrase *mille' acharei* (lit. "filled after") is a fixed idiom for complete loyalty. Og, Sichon, and the other excepts are not mentioned here — Moshe recounts the narrative selectively for rhetorical impact.

**v.37 — Also against me the LORD showed anger:** *gam bi hit'anaf YHWH* — The verb *hit'anaf* (Hithpa'el of *anaf*) means to become enraged. EMET notes that Moshe places his own exclusion in the same paragraph as the people's punishment, linking his fate to theirs. The actual cause (Numbers 20) is not rehearsed here; Moshe blames the people (*biglalkem* — "on your account," v.37).

**v.44 — as the bees do:** *ka'asher ta'asenah ha-devorim* — literally "as the bees do." A vivid naturalistic simile for the swarming, stinging attack of the Emorites. The LXX: *ὃν τρόπον ποιοῦσιν αἱ μέλισσαι* — exact.

**v.46 — many days, as the days that you dwelt:** *rabbim yamim ka'yamim asher yeshavtem* — the repetition is awkward in Hebrew and deliberate. EMET preserves it without smoothing. The duration at Kadesh is left imprecise — rabbinic tradition counts 19 years there out of the 40.

— EMET
